elements.sent-2411-001: The key innovation of DyCoke over its predecessors is to dynamically remove redundant tokens during the decoding stage, squeezing both the temporal (video frames) and spatial redundancy in visual tokens.
elements.sent-2411-002: Right: Efficiency and performance comparison of various training-free token pruning methods on MVBench \cite{li2024mvbench with LLaVA-OV-7B~\cite{llava-ov .}}
elements.sent-2411-003: DyCoke surpasses the SoTA counterparts (PruMerge~\cite{shang2024llava , FastV~\cite{fastv ), with 1.5$\times$ inference speedup, 1.4$\times$ memory reduction against the baseline, while still improving the performance.. \end{center ].}}}
elements.sent-2411-004: Video large language models (VLLMs) have significantly advanced recently in processing complex video content, yet their inference efficiency remains constrained because of the high computational cost stemming from the thousands of visual tokens generated from the video inputs.
elements.sent-2411-005: We empirically observe that, unlike single image inputs, VLLMs typically attend visual tokens from different frames at different decoding iterations, making a one-shot pruning strategy prone to removing important tokens by mistake.
elements.sent-2411-006: Motivated by this, we present DyCoke, a training-free token compression method to optimize token representation and accelerate VLLMs.
elements.sent-2411-007: DyCoke incorporates a plug-and-play temporal compression module to minimize temporal redundancy by merging redundant tokens across frames, and applies dynamic KV cache reduction to prune spatially redundant tokens selectively.
elements.sent-2411-008: It ensures high-quality inference by dynamically retaining the critical tokens at each decoding step.
elements.sent-2411-009: Extensive experimental results demonstrate that DyCoke can outperform the prior SoTA counterparts, achieving 1.5$\times$ inference speedup, 1.4$\times$ memory reduction against the baseline VLLM, while still improving the performance, with no training.
elements.sent-2411-010: Video large language models (VLLMs) have advanced significantly in understanding diverse video contexts, primarily because of their enhanced reasoning ability for complex multimodal information \cite{li2025llama, lin2023video, zhang2023video, li2024mvbench, li2023videochat, xu2024pllava, llava-ov, wang2024tarsier, cheng2024videollama .}
elements.sent-2411-011: Most current VLLMs rely on sequential visual representations.
elements.sent-2411-012: When dozens of video frames are fed into the language model along with a language prompt, the video input is converted into tens of thousands of tokens.
elements.sent-2411-013: Because of the quadratic complexity of attention mechanism, the inherent visual redundancy across video frames leads to a dramatic surge of computational complexity, resulting in prohibitive training and inference costs.
elements.sent-2411-014: Previous research \cite{chu2023mobilevlm, chu2024mobilevlm, yuan2023tinygpt, zhou2024tinyllava has largely focused on developing lighter large language models (LLMs) with fewer parameters; however, this often significantly diminishes the complex reasoning capabilities of these models.}
elements.sent-2411-015: Consequently, how to reduce the number of video tokens while maintaining model performance emerges as a new research direction.
elements.sent-2411-016: In previous works on token compression for image LLMs, the attention scores of visual tokens serve as the primary metric for assessing token importance, resulting in a single-stage pruning approach.
elements.sent-2411-017: For example, FastV \cite{fastv evaluates the distribution of attention between visual tokens and predicted tokens in the language model during the prefilling phase, leveraging the KV cache.}
elements.sent-2411-018: In contrast, LLaVA-PruMerge \cite{shang2024llava selects key visual tokens using attention scores derived from the CLIP visual encoder \cite{radford2021learning .}}
elements.sent-2411-019: However, the substantial amount of long-term sequential information stored in videos results in considerable temporal and spatial redundancy \cite{he2022masked,tong2022videomae,ren2023testa .}
elements.sent-2411-020: We present the distribution of the (averaged) attention scores for each predicted token over the visual tokens in \cref{fig-1 .}
elements.sent-2411-021: It shows that the overall distribution of attention scores between visual tokens is highly sparse, and the model’s focus shifts to different visual tokens as the decoding proceeds, resembling human attention patterns.
elements.sent-2411-022: Consequently, for video LLMs, unlike image inputs, a single-stage pruning strategy may result in incorrect token filtering, omission of key tokens, and temporal disarray, thereby compromising video comprehension.
elements.figure-2411-001: \captionsetup{font={small}, skip=-1pt} \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth, trim=0.4cm 0cm 2cm 0cm, clip]{Images/attention_heatmap.pdf} \caption{Attention score between the predicted token at different decoding iterations (x-axis) and the input video tokens (y-axis) at the decoding stage of LLaVA-OV-7B \cite{llava-ov} (attention score averaged over all attention layers). Note, some video tokens (e.g., frame \#1) become less important as the decoding proceeds, while others may instead become more important (e.g., frame \#16). This observation motivates us to develop DyCoke, a token compression method that can dynamically exploit the token redundancy during decoding.} \label{fig-1}
elements.sent-2411-023: Based on the above analysis, we reassess the need for a straightforward and effective token compression method tailored to VLLMs, using the significant temporal and spatial redundancy in video information as a foundational consideration.
elements.sent-2411-024: Thus, in this work, we present the first temporal-spatial dynamic token compression method (DyCoke) to optimize the token representation tailored for VLLMs, as illustrated in \cref{fig:teaser (left).}
elements.sent-2411-025: The first phase involves designing a plug-and-play, lightweight token compression module that addresses temporal redundancy by merging similar tokens across frames.
elements.sent-2411-026: We group consecutive frames by sampling and identify tokens with overlapping information in adjacent or nearby frames for temporal merging.
elements.sent-2411-027: The second phase maintains a parsimonious KV cache established in the first phase by dynamically pruning less important information, reducing the spatial redundancy visual tokens, while retaining pruned tokens for secondary activations needed for auxiliary computations.
elements.sent-2411-028: Specifically, our approach enables the model to dynamically select a distinct set of tokens at each decoding step, which is essential for preserving performance.
elements.sent-2411-029: Building on this, DyCoke maximizes reasoning ability while substantially reducing visual tokens, resulting in a more streamlined and representative visual token set.
elements.sent-2411-030: Empirically, the proposed DyCoke demonstrates excellent performance in video reasoning tasks, simplifying visual tokens as much as possible while maintaining model performance, particularly with long encoded inputs, and does not require fine-tuning or parameter modifications.
elements.sent-2411-031: In the first stage, after temporal merging, redundant visual tokens can be adaptively reduced by 50\% - 60\%.
elements.sent-2411-032: In the second stage, each iteration can dynamically further reduce visual tokens by an additional 70\% - 90\% based on the first stage.
elements.sent-2411-033: On average, each frame of video input retains 15 tokens for attention matrix calculation, greatly accelerating the inference process.
elements.sent-2411-034: As shown in \cref{fig:teaser (right), DyCoke achieves a 1.54$\times$ inference speedup on LLaVA-OV-7B \cite{llava-ov , with the lowest memory consumption and highest accuracy.}}
elements.sent-2411-036: Our contributions in this work are summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-037: We propose a plug-and-play temporal token merging block to effectively reduce the number of visual tokens while preserving key video content information by leveraging temporal redundancy between frames and merging similar tokens.
elements.sent-2411-038: We propose a dynamic KV cache token reduction method that dynamically reduces redundant tokens in the KV cache without relying on additional parameters or training, enabling efficient processing of long input sequences.
elements.sent-2411-039: Experimental results on several video inference benchmarks show that DyCoke maintains high inference accuracy and speed while compressing visual tokens and enables the processing of longer video sequences within the same computational budget.
elements.sent-2411-040: With advances in large language models (LLMs) and their strong multi-modal understanding and reasoning capabilities, many studies have attempted to integrate LLMs with video encoders to leverage these powerful capabilities for video tasks \cite{li2023videochat, li2024mvbench, llava-ov,wang2024tarsier, lin2023video,li2024llava, jin2024chat, liu2023video, luo2023valley, li2025llama, cheng2024videollama .}
elements.sent-2411-041: Representative works, such as VideoChat \cite{li2023videochat and VideoLLaMA \cite{li2025llama , use video converters to encode video features based on image LLMs, enhancing understanding capabilities by training on extensive video datasets.}}
elements.sent-2411-042: LLaVA-NeXT-Interleave \cite{li2024llava and LLaVA-OneVision \cite{llava-ov focus on achieving excellent performance across single-image, multi-image, and video scenarios.}}
elements.sent-2411-043: Although the potential of VLLMs for video understanding and reasoning is being realized, the tens of thousands of visual tokens required for long videos significantly increase both inference time and memory demands.
elements.sent-2411-044: While works such as VILA aim to optimize token usage, substantial hardware resources are still needed for model fine-tuning \cite{lin2024vila, wang2024tarsier, jin2024chat, li2025llama .}
elements.sent-2411-045: Therefore, we desire a token compression method specifically for VLLMs that requires no fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-046: While multi-modal large language models (MLLMs) have made significant progress \cite{huang2025lita, li2023blip, liu2024improved, liu2024visual, zhu2023minigpt , their large-scale training and deployment entail substantial computational costs.}
elements.sent-2411-047: LLaVA-1.5 \cite{liu2024improved,dettmers2022gpt3,shang2023pb addresses this by using 4-bit rather than 8-bit quantization for compression.}
elements.sent-2411-048: MobileVLM \cite{chu2023mobilevlm and MobilevLM-v2 \cite{chu2024mobilevlm utilize compact architectures optimized for mobile applications.}}
elements.sent-2411-049: Additionally, TinyGPT-V \cite{yuan2023tinygpt , LLaVA-Phi \cite{zhu2024llava , and Vary-toy \cite{wei2024small aim to match or exceed the performance of large models by using smaller LLM backbones, such as Phi-2 \cite{javaheripi2023phi .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-050: MoE-LLaVA \cite{lin2024moe employs a mixture of experts to address model sparsity and improve both efficiency and performance.}
elements.sent-2411-051: TinyLLaVA \cite{zhou2024tinyllava explores more lightweight MLLM architectures and training optimizations.}
elements.sent-2411-052: However, in most cases, reducing the size of the LMM backbone compromises its reasoning capability.
elements.sent-2411-053: Improving LMMs efficiency by compressing the number of visual tokens offers a promising alternative.
elements.sent-2411-054: In previous studies, token pruning has been widely adopted to mitigate token redundancy in vision transformers (ViTs) and large language models (LLMs).
elements.sent-2411-055: ToMe \cite{bolya2022token propose merging similar tokens within ViTs to consolidate redundant information while preserving task-relevant content across various domains like image, video, and audio processing.}
elements.sent-2411-056: TESTA \cite{ren2023testa achieves up to a 75\% reduction in processed tokens through the use of temporal and spatial aggregation modules.}
elements.sent-2411-057: And TempMe \cite{shen2024tempme address temporal redundancy by progressively merging tokens across neighboring clips.}
elements.sent-2411-058: FastV \cite{chen2024image enhances attention efficiency in MLLMs by pruning redundant image tokens based on attention scores, without requiring additional training.}
elements.sent-2411-059: LLaVA-PruMerge \cite{shang2024llava introduces adaptive token reduction by selecting key visual tokens based on attention scores derived from the CLIP visual encoder.}
elements.sent-2411-060: Furthermore, Look-m \cite{wan2024look apply token merging strategies in KV cache to decrease computational costs and support extended multimodal contexts.}
elements.sent-2411-061: LazyLLM \cite{fu2024lazyllm employ attention maps to progressively prune tokens, reducing time-to-first-token (TTFT).}
elements.sent-2411-062: In our study, we present a novel, training-free dynamic token compression strategy specifically designed for VLLMs that fully accounts for the temporal characteristics of visual tokens to preserve model performance as effectively as possible.
elements.figure-2411-002: \captionsetup{font={small}, skip=8pt} \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{Images/MED-MAIN-5.pdf} \caption{Detailed overview of our DyCoke method. DyCoke compresses visual tokens in VLLMs through a two-stage pruning process: visual token temporal merging (TTM) and KV cache dynamic pruning. Token temporal merging (illustrated in the \textcolor{red}{red} dashed box on the left) merges similar tokens in video frames at the prefilling stage, tapping into the temporal redundancy of the video input; KV cache dynamic pruning (illustrated in the \textcolor{blue}{blue} dashed box on the right) further removes less attended visual tokens in the KV cache dynamically at the decoding stage, exploiting the spatial redundancy in visual tokens. Note, DyCoke is a drop-in training-free approach to accelerate VLLMs.} \label{fig-MED}
elements.sent-2411-063: Video LLM inference typically comprises two stages: prefilling and decoding.. (1) Prefilling Stage.
elements.sent-2411-064: In the prefilling stage, for a video with $M_v$ frames, the image encoder maps each frame into $K_v$ embedding vectors, $\mathbf{z _i\in\mathbb{R ^{K_v\times d_v}$, where $d_v$ is the dimension of each embedding vector.}}
elements.sent-2411-065: The $M_v$ frames thus form a embedding sequence $\mathbf{Z_v =\left[\mathbf{z _1,\mathbf{z _2,...,\mathbf{z _{M_v}\right]$, which is fed into the projector, which maps visual tokens into the same feature space as text to facilitate information fusion and alignment across modalities.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-066: The projector output is processed to generate the set $H_{v'}\in\mathbb{R ^{M_vN_{v}\times D}$ of visual tokens, where $N_v$ is the token length corresponding to one frame of video.}
elements.sent-2411-067: Simultaneously, the model receives a token sequence prompt $T = \{ t_i \}_{i=1 ^{N_q}$, where $t_i$ represents the $i$-th token of $N_q$ tokens in total.}
elements.sent-2411-068: We can get the set of text tokens $H_{q}\in\mathbb{R ^{N_{q}\times D}$ where $D$ is the dimension of hidden state.}
elements.sent-2411-069: Next, the visual tokens and text tokens are concatenated as LLM input, $H=\text{concat [H_{v' , H_q]$.}}
elements.sent-2411-070: In each transformer layer $l$ of an LLM, self-attention is applied to $H$.
elements.sent-2411-071: This process involves computing the query $\mathbf{Q ^{l}$, key $\mathbf{K ^{l}$, and value $\mathbf{V ^{l}$ matrices using linear transformations.}}}
elements.sent-2411-072: Specifically, \begin{equation}\mathbf{Q}^{l} = H \mathbf{W}_Q^{l}, \quad \mathbf{K}^{l} = H \mathbf{W}_K^{l}, \quad \mathbf{V}^{l} = H \mathbf{W}_V^{l},\end{equation} where \( \mathbf{W _Q^{l , \mathbf{W _K^{l , \mathbf{W _V^{l \in \mathbb{R ^{D \times D \) are learnable projection matrices.}}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}Q^{l} = H W_Q^{l}, \quad K^{l} = H W_K^{l}, \quad V^{l} = H W_V^{l},\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-073: These transformations project the input into a latent space where attention can be efficiently calculated.
elements.sent-2411-074: The $K$ and $V$ matrices are computed and subsequently stored in the KV cache to facilitate token generation during decoding.. (2) Decoding Stage.
elements.sent-2411-075: In the decoding phase, the model sequentially generates tokens by using and updating the KV cache stored during the prefilling phase.
elements.sent-2411-076: At each time step $t$, only the key and value of the new token $h_i$ are computed, without recalculating the attention for the entire sequence.
elements.sent-2411-077: The $\mathbf{K}$ and $\mathbf{V}$ values calculated for the new token are updated in the KV cache: \begin{equation}\mathbf{K}=[\mathbf{K},h_{t}\mathbf{W}_{K}],\mathbf{V}=[\mathbf{V},h_{t}\mathbf{W}_{V}],\end{equation} which significantly reduces the computational load.
elements.sent-2411-078: DyCoke employs a two-stage token compression strategy.
elements.sent-2411-079: The first stage merges visual tokens that exhibit significant temporal redundancy across frames, and the second stage then dynamically prunes visual tokens for attention weight calculation, building on the first stage.
elements.sent-2411-080: This approach aims to preserve model performance while simplifying the tokens as extensively as possible.
elements.sent-2411-081: In VLLMs, the video input contains significant temporal redundancy.
elements.sent-2411-082: Some activities often persist across multiple frames with minimal visual change, while backgrounds and stationary objects frequently contain similar information across frames, causing substantial redundancy.
elements.sent-2411-083: Combining these redundant visual tokens at temporal scales can reduce the total token length of the input, which accelerates VLLMs inference and decreases memory consumption.
elements.sent-2411-084: We introduce a plug-and-play token temporal merging (TTM) module as a first-stage solution to filter out consecutive, redundant visual tokens.
elements.sent-2411-085: First, we assume that for an input visual token $H_v'$, the goal is to reduce $k\%$ of tokens through the merge operation.
elements.sent-2411-086: To achieve this, we compute the similarity between all possible token pairs and merge those with the highest similarity.
elements.sent-2411-087: However, this approach significantly increases computational load and processing time, making the cost outweigh the benefits.
elements.sent-2411-088: Therefore, given the high incidence of temporal redundancy between adjacent frames, we employ continuous sampling of visual tokens corresponding to the input video frames.
elements.sent-2411-089: As shown in \cref{fig-MED , TTM initially performs uniform sampling with a sliding window with a length of 4 frames, dividing tokens into groups O (Odd) and E (Even) and calculating token similarity between corresponding positions in adjacent groups.}
elements.sent-2411-090: We use cosine similarity to calculate token similarity $\mathcal{S}$: \begin{equation}\mathcal{S} =\cos(\theta)=\frac{h_{i}\cdot h_{j}}{\|h_{i}\|\|h_{j}\|}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-091: We prune tokens in group E with high similarity to those in group O.
elements.sent-2411-092: We then calculate the similarity between frames within group O, retaining the full token of the first frame in the sampling window and pruning the remaining tokens.
elements.sent-2411-093: The pruning rate in TTM is set to $k\%$, and this process is repeated for each subsequent sampling window.
elements.sent-2411-094: Finally, the LLM input can be redefined as \begin{equation}H = \text{concat}[\mathrm{TTM}(H_{v'}), H_q].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-095: At this stage, TTM enables visual marker reduction by leveraging temporal dependencies.
elements.sent-2411-096: The TTM module is simple, effective, and plug-and-play, with a negligible processing time of less than $10^{-3}$ seconds for 32 input frames.
elements.sent-2411-097: To further compress visual tokens, we analyze the distribution of average attention scores for the visual token represented by \cref{fig-1 for each predicted token.}
elements.sent-2411-098: Results indicate that the attention score of the visual token for the next prediction token is highly sparse, suggesting substantial redundancy in the input visual token that can be safely pruned without impacting the next prediction.
elements.sent-2411-099: We also observe that each prediction token focuses on a different visual token at various decoding stages.
elements.sent-2411-100: This observation aligns with the human process of understanding long-sequence video information, leading us to consider a dynamic pruning scheme for the KV cache during decoding.
elements.sent-2411-101: At the first decoding iteration, for an LLM with $N_{lm}$ layers, we compute the cross-attention weights between the predicted token and the visual token at layer $L$ to calculate the average attention score matrix: \begin{equation}\mathbf{A}^{(L)}=\mathrm{Softmax}\left(\frac{\mathbf{Q}^{(L)}(\mathbf{K}^{(L)})^{\top}}{\sqrt{D}}\right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-102: We then extract the attention scores of visual tokens and predicted tokens to form a subset $\mathbf{A _v^{(L)}$.}
elements.sent-2411-103: To obtain the top $p\%$ attention scores in the cross-attention matrix $\mathbf{A _v^{(L)}$, we calculate a threshold $\tau$ and define the set $\mathcal{I _p^{(L)}$ comprising the indices of these top $p\%$ attention scores.}}
elements.sent-2411-104: Then we prune the visual tokens in the KV cache, retain tokens with the high attention scores, and update the KV cache: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \mathbf{K}_{v}^{(L)}=\{\mathbf{K}_v^{(L)}[i]\mid i\in\mathcal{I}p^{(L)}\}, \mathbf{V}_v^{(L)}=\{\mathbf{V}_v^{(L)}[i]\mid i\in\mathcal{I}_{p}^{(L)}\}.\end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\mathbf{K _{v ^{(L)}$ and $\mathbf{V _v^{(L)}$ denote the set of visual tokens in the KV cache of layer $L$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-105: In the next decoding step, the model may need to refocus on tokens that were previously pruned.
elements.sent-2411-106: If they are discarded directly, the model cannot retrieve their KV cache entries.
elements.sent-2411-107: We also consider that tokens of interest tend to remain consistent across successive iterations.
elements.sent-2411-108: To reduce large-scale indexing requirements, we introduce a dynamic pruning cache (DP cache) to store pruned tokens, which can be denoted as \begin{equation}\begin{gathered}K_{DP}^{(L)}=\{\mathbf{K}^{(L)}[i]\mid i\in\mathcal{J}^{(L)}\}, \mathbf{V}_{DP}^{(L)}=\{\mathbf{V}^{(L)}[i]\mid i\in\mathcal{J}^{(L)}\},\end{gathered}\end{equation} where $\mathcal{J ^{(L) =\{i\mid i\notin\mathcal{I p^{(L)}\}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-109: In the next decoding iteration, the cross-attention matrix is recalculated at layer $L$, dynamically adding tokens from the DP cache with increased attention scores into the calculation and storing them in the KV cache.
elements.sent-2411-110: Simultaneously, tokens whose attention scores have decreased since the previous stage and no longer fall within the top $p\%$ are indexed.
elements.sent-2411-111: These tokens are removed from the KV cache and stored in the DP cache.
elements.sent-2411-112: This process repeats at each decoding stage, with the KV cache and DP cache dynamically updated for optimal token compression.

elements.sent-2411-121: These samples require the model to choose the correct answer from multiple provided options.. Computing Cost Evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-122: We examine the total FLOPs of the prefilling stage and the decoding stage.
elements.sent-2411-123: For a transformer layer with multi-head attention (MHA) and feed-forward network (FFN) modules, let $n$ represent the number of tokens, $d$ be the hidden state size, and $m$ be the intermediate size of FFN.
elements.sent-2411-124: In the prefilling phase, the total FLOPs can be estimated as $4nd^2 + 2n^2d + 2ndm$.
elements.sent-2411-125: For the decoding phase, considering the significant contribution of the KV cache, the computational consumption for $R$ total iterations (i.e., predicting $R$ tokens) is $R\left(4d^2+2dm\right)+2\sum_{i=1 ^{R d\times(n+i)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-126: We unify $R=100$ for calculation in the experiments.
elements.sent-2411-127: Thus, for an LLM with $T$ total transformer layers, the total FLOPs can be expressed as follows, \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \mathrm{FLOPs} =T(4nd^2+2n^2d+2ndm) +TR\left((4d^2+2dm)+2\left(dn+\frac{d(R+1)}{2}\right)\right). \end{aligned}\end{equation} Comparison Methods.
elements.sent-2411-128: We compare our method with two latest training-free visual token compression methods: LLaVA-PruMerge~\cite{shang2024llava leverages the sparsity of attention scores in CLIP \cite{radford2021learning to identify key tokens; FastV~\cite{fastv adopts the attention score between the predicted token and the visual tokens during the prefilling stage to identify key tokens.}}}
elements.sent-2411-129: Notably, FastV offers two versions, with and without KV cache.
elements.sent-2411-130: In practice, we found that FastV without KV cache causes a sharp increase in inference time for video input, thereby we mainly compare to the version with KV cache.

elements.sent-2411-169: This paper presents DyCoke, a new training-free method to dynamically reduce the visual tokens for faster video large language models (VLLMs).
elements.sent-2411-170: We develop a two-stage token compression strategy that leverages the temporal and spatial redundancy in video information: In the first stage, highly similar temporal tokens among frames are merged; the second stage further reduces the visual tokens used for attention computation during decoding stage.
elements.sent-2411-171: To the best of our knowledge, this is the first dynamic token pruning method specifically tailored for VLLMs.
elements.sent-2411-172: Extensive benchmark and analysis results on a wide range of video QA and reasoning tasks with three VLLMs (0.5B, 7B, 70B parameters) show our method consistently surpasses the prior SoTA counterparts.

elements.sent-2411-176: To complement the illustration, we provide a brief description of the 20 tasks included in the MVBench dataset.
elements.sent-2411-177: The MVBench dataset focuses on evaluating the model’s temporal reasoning ability, spanning basic perceptual to advanced cognitive tasks across nine broad categories, including complex tasks such as action recognition, object localization, and scene transformation.
elements.sent-2411-178: Each task requires the model to handle dynamic changes in video sequences, compensating for the limitations in temporal understanding found in existing still-image tasks.
elements.sent-2411-179: For example, in the “action” task, the model must recognize action sequences, predict future actions, and make fine distinctions between similar actions to achieve a nuanced understanding of human behavior in videos.
elements.sent-2411-180: Additionally, MVBench includes tasks involving object interaction and state changes, such as determining whether an object is present in a video or identifying object position changes over different time periods.
elements.sent-2411-181: The dataset also includes high-level cognitive tasks such as “counterfactual reasoning” and “episodic reasoning,” requiring the model to speculate on causality in complex situations and navigate based on an egocentric perspective.
elements.sent-2411-182: The 20 tasks in the \cref{tab:mvbench are: AS (action sequence), AP (action prediction), AA (action antonymy), FA (fine-grained action), UA (unexpected action), OE (object existence), OI (object interaction), OS (object shuffle), MD (movement direction), AL (action localization), ST (scene transition), AC (action counting), MC (movement counting), MA (movement attributes), SC (state change), FP (fine-grained pose), CO (character order), EN (egocentric navigation), ER (episodic reasoning), and CI (counterfactual inference).}

elements.sent-2411-191: DyCoke surpasses the SoTA counterparts (PruMerge~\cite{shang2024llava , FastV~\cite{fastv ), with 1.5$\times$ inference speedup and a 1.4$\times$ reduction in memory usage relative to the baseline, while simultaneously enhancing performance.. \end{center ].}}}
elements.sent-2411-192: Video large language models (VLLMs) have significantly advanced recently in processing complex video content.
elements.sent-2411-193: Yet, their inference efficiency remains constrained because of the high computational cost stemming from the thousands of visual tokens generated from the video inputs.
elements.sent-2411-194: We empirically observe that, unlike single image inputs, VLLMs typically attend visual tokens from different frames at different decoding iterations.
elements.sent-2411-195: This makes a one-shot pruning strategy prone to removing important tokens by mistake.
elements.sent-2411-196: DyCoke incorporates a plug-and-play temporal compression module to minimize temporal redundancy by merging redundant tokens across frames and applying dynamic KV cache reduction to prune spatially redundant tokens selectively.
elements.sent-2411-197: Extensive experimental results demonstrate that DyCoke can outperform the prior SoTA counterparts, achieving 1.5$\times$ inference speedup, and 1.4$\times$ memory reduction against the baseline VLLM, while still improving the performance, with no training.
elements.sent-2411-198: Because of the quadratic complexity of the attention mechanism, the inherent visual redundancy across video frames leads to a dramatic surge of computational complexity, resulting in prohibitive training and inference costs.
elements.figure-2411-013: \captionsetup{font={small}, skip=-1pt} \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth, trim=0.4cm 0cm 2cm 0cm, clip]{Images/attention_heatmap.pdf} \caption{Attention score between the predicted token at different decoding iterations (x-axis) and the input video tokens (y-axis) at the decoding stage of LLaVA-OV-7B \cite{llava-ov} (attention score averaged over all attention layers). Note, that some video tokens (e.g., frame \#1) become less important as the decoding proceeds, while others may instead become more important (e.g., frame \#16). This observation motivates us to develop DyCoke, a training-free plug-and-play token compression method that can dynamically exploit the token redundancy during decoding.} \label{fig-1}
elements.sent-2411-199: We group consecutive frames by sampling and identifying tokens with overlapping information in adjacent or nearby frames for temporal merging.
elements.sent-2411-200: The second phase maintains a parsimonious KV cache established in the first phase by dynamically pruning less important information, reducing the spatial redundancy of visual tokens, while retaining pruned tokens for secondary activations needed for auxiliary computations.
elements.sent-2411-201: On average, each video input frame retains 15 tokens for attention matrix calculation, greatly accelerating the inference process.
elements.sent-2411-202: We propose a plug-and-play temporal token merging block to effectively reduce visual tokens while preserving the key video content information by leveraging temporal redundancy between frames and merging similar tokens.
elements.sent-2411-203: Experimental results on several video inference benchmarks show that DyCoke maintains high inference accuracy and speed while compressing visual tokens and enables processing longer video sequences within the same computational budget.
elements.sent-2411-204: With advances in large language models (LLMs) and their strong multi-modal understanding and reasoning capabilities, many studies have attempted to integrate LLMs with video encoders to leverage these powerful capabilities for video tasks \cite{li2023videochat, li2024mvbench, llava-ov,wang2024tarsier, lin2023video,li2024llava, jin2024chat, liu2023video, luo2023valley, li2025llama, cheng2024videollama,li2024TP2O,Xiong2024ATIH,weng2024MambaLLIE .}
elements.sent-2411-205: While multi-modal large language models (MLLMs) have made significant progress \cite{huang2025lita, li2023blip, liu2024improved, liu2024visual, zhu2023minigpt,xiao2023robustmq,jiang2024effectiveness,jiang2024effectiveness,xiao2023robustmq,gao2024mini,huang2024empirical , their large-scale training and deployment entail substantial computational costs.}
elements.sent-2411-206: However, many of these works have noted that reducing the size of the LMM model backbone often compromises its reasoning capability.
elements.sent-2411-207: Improving LMM efficiency by compressing the number of visual tokens offers a promising alternative.
elements.sent-2411-208: In previous studies, token pruning has been widely adopted to mitigate token redundancy in vision transformers (ViTs) and large language models (LLMs) \cite{zhuang2025st3, fastv .}

elements.figure-2411-014: \captionsetup{font={small}, skip=8pt} \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{Images/method_final.pdf} \caption{Detailed overview of our DyCoke method. DyCoke compresses visual tokens in VLLMs through a two-stage pruning process: visual token temporal merging (TTM) and KV cache dynamic pruning. Token temporal merging (illustrated in the \textcolor{red}{red} dashed box on the left) merges similar tokens in video frames at the prefilling stage, tapping into the temporal redundancy of the video input; KV cache dynamic pruning (illustrated in the \textcolor{blue}{blue} dashed box on the right) further removes less attended visual tokens in the KV cache dynamically at the decoding stage, exploiting the spatial redundancy in visual tokens. DyCoke is a drop-in training-free approach to accelerate VLLMs.} \label{fig-MED}

elements.section-2411-011: Ablation Study about $k$ and Input Frames
elements.figure-2411-016: \captionsetup{font={small}, skip=6pt} \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth,height=0.4\linewidth]{Images/plot_rebuttal.pdf} \caption{Performance vs. $K$ values in different input frames.} \label{fig_R:1}
elements.sent-2411-238: As shown in \cref{fig_R:1 , we investigate the relationship among the numerical value of $k$, the number of input frames (32, 16, and 8), and the overall model performance.}
elements.sent-2411-239: Our results indicate that with a low number of input frames, token compression consistently leads to a decline in model performance.
elements.sent-2411-240: However, as the number of input frames increases—that is, as the multiplicity of visual tokens grows—the adverse impact of token compression on model performance gradually diminishes, eventually outperforming the baseline model.
elements.sent-2411-241: This phenomenon arises because more input frames introduce increased information redundancy and noise, which can be mitigated through moderate token compression, thereby maintaining performance with a slight enhancement.
elements.sent-2411-242: Flash Attention requires additional computation during the inference stage to compute the attention score matrix.
elements.sent-2411-243: However, combining Dycoke with Flash Attention does not impose significant additional computational overhead, as the attention score is computed only at a specific layer during each decoding iteration.
elements.sent-2411-244: Moreover, the computational complexity is substantially lower than that of the prefilling phase.
elements.sent-2411-245: DyCoke marks the first significant advancement in dynamic token pruning to improve inference efficiency in video large language models (VLLMs), yet some challenges remain for further exploration.
elements.sent-2411-246: Firstly, although DyCoke’s compression strategy effectively reduces token redundancy, specific video contexts (e.g., rapid scene changes or critical time shifts) may still incur minor information loss.
elements.sent-2411-247: While the dynamic token selection mechanism mitigates this risk, future work will focus on developing more fine-grained token compression methods for highly dynamic video content.
elements.sent-2411-248: Secondly, although token compression reduces memory consumption and enhances reasoning speed, fully deploying LLMs on mobile devices remains challenging due to their scale.
elements.sent-2411-249: Thus, we aim to integrate advanced compression techniques, such as quantization and distillation, to develop more efficient VLLMs.
